title: I Am Malala

passage.title: I Am Malala (excerpt)
passage.author: By Malala Yousafzai, with Christina Lamb
passage.text:
**In this memoir, Yousafzai describes how she survived a murder attempt by the Pakistani [word] for her advocacy for girls’ education. In the excerpt, Yousafzai recounts some childhood difficulties.**

1. Around that time we moved away from where we had been living on the same street as [word] to an area where I didn't have any friends. On our new road there was a girl called Safina, who was a bit younger than me, and we started to play together. She was a __pampered__ girl who had lots of dolls and a shoebox full of jewelry. But she kept eyeing up the pink plastic pretend mobile phone my father had bought me, which was one of the only toys I had. My father was always talking on his mobile so I loved to copy him and pretend to make calls on mine. One day it disappeared.

2. A few days later I saw Safina playing with a phone exactly the same as mine. "Where did you get that?" I asked. “I bought it in the [word],” she said.

3. I realize now she could have been telling the truth but back then I thought, *She is doing this to me and I will do the same to her*. I used to go to her house to study, so whenever I was there I would pocket her things, mostly toy jewelry like earrings and necklaces. It was easy. At first stealing gave me a thrill, but that did not last long. Soon it became a [word]. I did not know how to stop.

4. One afternoon I came home from school and rushed into the kitchen as usual for a snack. “Hello, *[word]*!" I called. "I'm starving!" There was silence. My mother was sitting on the floor pounding spices, brightly colored turmeric and cumin, filling the air with their aroma. Over and over she pounded. Her eyes would not meet mine. What had I done? I was very sad and went to my room. When I opened my cupboard, I saw that all the things I had taken were gone. I had been caught.

5. My cousin Reena came into my room. "They knew you were stealing," she said. "They were waiting for you to come clean, but you just kept on."

6. I felt a terrible sinking feeling in my stomach. I walked back to my mother with my head bowed. "What you did was wrong, Malala," she said. "Are you trying to bring shame on us that we can't afford to buy such things?"

7. "It's not true!" I lied. "I didn't take them."

8. But she knew I had. "Safina started it," I protested. “She took the pink phone that Aba bought me."

9. My mother was unmoved. "Safina is younger than you and you should have taught her better," she said. "You should have set an example.”

10. I started crying and apologized over and over again. "Don't tell Aba," I begged. I couldn't bear for him to be disappointed in me. It's horrible to feel unworthy in the eyes of your parents.

11. It wasn't the first time. When I was little I went to the bazaar with my mother and spotted a pile of almonds on a cart. They looked so tasty that I couldn't resist grabbing a handful. My mother told me off and apologized to the cart owner. He was furious and would not be [word]. We still had little money and my mother checked her purse to see what she had. "Can you sell them to me for ten [word]?" she asked. "No," he replied. "Almonds are very costly."

12. My mother was very upset and told my father. He immediately went and bought the whole lot from the man and put them in a glass dish.

13. "Almonds are good,” he said. "If you eat them with milk just before bed it makes you brainy." But I knew he didn't have much money and the almonds in the dish were a reminder of my guilt. I promised myself I'd never do such a thing again. And now I had. My mother took me to say sorry to Safina and her parents. It was very hard. Safina said nothing about my phone, which didn't seem fair, but I didn't mention it either.

14. Though I felt bad, I was also relieved it was over. Since that day I have never lied or stolen. Not a single lie nor a single penny [...]

15. My mother and father tell each other everything, so Aba soon found out why I was so sad. I could see in his eyes that I had failed him. I wanted him to be proud of me, like he was when I was presented with the first-in-year trophies at school. Or the day our kindergarten teacher Miss Ulfat told him I had written "Only Speak in [word]" on the blackboard for my classmates at the start of an Urdu lesson so we would learn the language faster. Or when I came first in the [word] essay writing competition.

16. My father [word] me by telling me about the mistakes great heroes made when they were children. He told me that [word] said, “Freedom is not worth having if it does not include the freedom to make mistakes."

questions.question_content: **Which context clue from paragraph 1 best helps readers determine the meaning of *pampered*?**

choices.label: B
choices.content: “who was a bit younger than me”

choices.label: C
choices.content: “lots of dolls and a shoebox full of jewelry”

note: KA serves 1/4 questions to anonymous users. Passage and sample question captured for skeleton generation.
